However I performed some investigations and was able to track down the problem by adding `traceback.print_exc()` in the relevant python code. What solved the problem was the following

* Install eventlet and gevent package in the virtualenv (the missing modules seemed to prevent Flask-SocketIO to load the async mode submodule)
* I manually applied the patch (found by googlingat [https://bugs.python.org/file45163/cygwin-pyio-setmode.patch](https://bugs.python.org/file45163/cygwin-pyio-setmode.patch))
```
diff -r 74eb9c51d64e Lib/_pyio.py
--- a/Lib/_pyio.py	Thu Oct 20 16:33:39 2016 -0400
+++ b/Lib/_pyio.py	Fri Oct 21 06:38:50 2016 +0900
@@ -13,8 +13,13 @@
     from _thread import allocate_lock as Lock
 except ImportError:
     from _dummy_thread import allocate_lock as Lock
-if sys.platform in {'win32', 'cygwin'}:
+if sys.platform == 'win32':
     from msvcrt import setmode as _setmode
+elif sys.platform == 'cygwin':
+    import ctypes
+    _cygwin1 = ctypes.PyDLL('cygwin1.dll')
+    def _setmode(fd, mode):
+        return _cygwin1._setmode(ctypes.c_int(fd), ctypes.c_int(mode))
 else:
     _setmode = None
```  
This fixed the exception that the module 'msvcrt' was not found during load of 'eventlet'. Then the message 'failed to initialize socketio app with async mode "eventlet". Continuing with async mode "threading".' of gdbgui disappeared.
* Finally I added the flag 'use_reloader=False' in the 'socketio.run' command on line 131 in backend.py. Thie prevented the restarting (which I do not understand, why it happend)  and premature termination of gdbgui. Then I was able to open  gdbgui in webbrowser and to debug a program :-)

I don't see how to make attaching the generator to the task work -- it's legal to start a generator in one task and then pass it to another, right? What if the parent task exits while the generator is still in use?

[As you may remember](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/#cleanup-in-generators-and-async-generators) I'm really really not a fan of the async generator gc design, exactly because it creates these kinds of issues. I mean, it's fine for asyncio, because asyncio just gives up on worrying about implicit concurrency, context preservation, exception propagation, etc. But in curio, relying on gc to clean up generators basically breaks every invariant in curio's API. (I mean, just to start, notice that it's a way to spawn a task from sync code...) It also makes it very easy to write code that will work OK on CPython, but then on PyPy they'll pass their testsuite but blow up under load, which is unhealthy for the ecosystem -- even if I know *I'm* careful about cleaning up resources, I have to assume that random libraries on PyPI are all broken...

My preference would be to document that this is a flaw in the language and that you need to use `async with` blocks on your async generators, and only use the hooks to detect cases when people forgot to do this and issue noisy warnings suggesting that they add `async with` blocks. And hope for [PEP 533](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0533/) or similar to be implemented for 3.7. (Which is more likely if we stick fast to our principles instead of implementing half-working workarounds.)

Not that this solves the issue of taking an i/o hit, but in the meanwhile a safe way to concatenate operations is to use ```tempfile```:

```python
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix='.wav') as tmp:

    tfm = sox.Transformer()
    tfm.trim(0, 5)
    tfm.build(infile, tmp.name)

    cbn = sox.Combiner()
    cbn.build([tmp.name, tmp.name], outfile, 'concatenate')
```
The downside is that since sox requires a filename, you need to use a ```NamedTemporaryFile``` and that means the system will think the file already exists when you call ```build()```, which means you'll always get an overwrite warning (which perhaps could be disabled by the addition of an optional flag @rabitt ?)

Right now the workaround is by changing the logger level:

```python
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel('CRITICAL')
    
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix='.wav') as tmp:

    tfm = sox.Transformer()
    tfm.trim(0, 5)
    tfm.build(infile, tmp.name)

    cbn = sox.Combiner()
    cbn.build([tmp.name, tmp.name], outfile, 'concatenate')

logger.setLevel('WARNING')
```
Or if you just want to suppress all logging you can do ```logger.disabled = True```

Regarding performance: if you turn on markdown support in csv table, each cell is parsed from markdown to native individually, and each time it called pandoc once. So this is an overhead of calling an externel executables (the starup time there.) This limitation comes from the fact that "there's no real pandoc library in Python", by this I mean all existing Python pandoc library actually calls the pandoc cli program with a bunch of commands, rather than e.g. using the C FFI and build a proper C module calling Haskell library.

We need to remember to be careful with using a dictionary as a default for a function argument -- i.e., we mustn't modify it in the function in any way, since it is mutable. I remember some gotcha from when I was new at Python :P :

```python
>>> def my_func(dct={'a': 1, 'b': 2}, some_pair=('c', 1)):
...     dct[some_pair[0]] = some_pair[1] 
...     return dct

>>> res1 = my_func()
>>> print(res1)
{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 1}

>>> res2 = my_func(some_pair=('d', 2))
>>> print(res2)
{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 1, 'd': 2}
```

```python
import operator

import numpy as np


FIRST_ITEM = operator.itemgetter(0)
EPS = 1e-12
TO_QUAD_COEFFS = np.array([
    [ 1.0, -2.0, 1.0],
    [-2.0,  2.0, 0.0],
    [ 1.0,  0.0, 0.0],
])


def quad_roots(coeffs):
    # Pretend to be roots of a quadratic
    rts = np.roots(coeffs)
    if rts.size == 2:
        s1, s2 = rts
    elif rts.size == 1:
        s1 = s2 = rts[0]
    elif rts.size > 2:
        raise ValueError('Unexpected number of roots.')
    else:
        # no roots at all
        s1 = s2 = 1.0j  # Fake roots as imaginary (will be rejected).
    return s1, s2


def point_on_curve(control_pts, pt):
    zero_control = control_pts - pt
    coeffs = TO_QUAD_COEFFS.dot(zero_control)

    s1x, s2x = quad_roots(coeffs[:, 0])
    if s1x.imag != 0.0:  # Either both are real or neither.
        return np.nan
    s1y, s2y = quad_roots(coeffs[:, 1])
    if s1y.imag != 0.0:  # Either both are real or neither.
        return np.nan

    rel1 = EPS * np.abs(s1x)
    if np.abs(s1x - s1y) <= rel1:
        return s1x
    if np.abs(s1x - s2y) <= rel1:
        return s1x
    rel2 = EPS * np.abs(s2x)
    if np.abs(s2x - s1y) <= rel2:
        return s2x
    if np.abs(s2x - s2y) <= rel2:
        return s2x

    return np.nan


def check_identical_curve(control1, control2, i1, i2,
                          intersections, intersection_params):
    # point_on_curve() <---> Curve.locate() BUT allow outside of [0, 1]
    s0 = point_on_curve(control1, control2[0, :])
    if np.isnan(s0):
        return False
    s2 = point_on_curve(control1, control2[2, :])
    if np.isnan(s2):
        return False

    transform1 = np.array([
        [1.0 - s0, s0, 0.0],
        [0.0, 1.0 - s0, s0],
    ])
    transform2 = np.array([
        [1.0 - s2, s2],
    ])
    # This is the middle node after doing
    # Curve.specialize(s1, s2)
    new_p1 = transform2.dot(transform1.dot(control1))
    if not vector_close(new_p1, control2[[1], :]):
        return False

    # Currently we have
    # s = s0 (control1) <--> s = 0 (control2)
    # s = s2            <--> s = 1
    # which implies that
    # s = 0             <--> s = (0 - s0) / (s2 - s0)
    # s = 1             <--> s = (1 - s0) / (s2 - s0)
    ds = s2 - s0
    # Now we need to limit the parameters {s0, s2, 0, 1}
    # inside [0, 1].
    min_info, max_info = sorted([(s0, 0.0, control2[0, :]),
                                 (s2, 1.0, control2[2, :])], key=FIRST_ITEM)
    min_s = min_info[0]
    max_s = max_info[0]
    # If the intervals intersect at all, add two intersections.
    if min_s < 1 + EPS and max_s > -EPS:
        if min_s > 0.0:
            intersections.append(min_info[2])
            add_intersection_params(min_s, i1, min_info[1], i2,
                                    intersection_params)
        else:
            intersections.append(control1[0, :])
            s_val = -s0 / ds
            add_intersection_params(0.0, i1, s_val, i2,
                                    intersection_params)

        if max_s < 1.0:
            intersections.append(max_info[2])
            add_intersection_params(max_s, i1, max_info[1], i2,
                                    intersection_params)
        else:
            intersections.append(control1[2, :])
            s_val = (1 - s0) / ds
            add_intersection_params(1.0, i1, s_val, i2,
                                    intersection_params)
    return True
```

Well, I use py.test for testing. Every module has a test folder, with quite a few tests written already. 

However, as mentioned a lot of tests are visual in nature (run a filter, plot against measurements), which of course is useless for automated testing.

I still stand by what I wrote above - we need to use statistical measures to detect if the filters are performing correctly or not.  And I strongly suspect that whatever measure is used should be part of the core library, since if it is useful to the library, it will be equally useful to users of the library to test the performance of their filters.

Then there is more simple kinds of tests - does the code work if passed a list instead of an array(if it should). If it works with a list, does it work with a tuple? Does it properly raise an exception if passed an array of the wrong shape? Are the outputs and object variables shaped correctly? Stuff like that.

As an aside, I'm curious about your use. As a pedagogical tool, I chose to write everything in Python. For performance, cython would be a better choice, but then the code becomes more or less unreadable for most people. I use FilterPy in my professional work, but only for analysis and prototyping - everything that runs in production is pure C++.

In any case, I welcome a contribution. If you feel you can tackle the statistical component, by all means give it a go. However, there is a still unwritten chapter in my book on performance measurement, so know I'd be pretty heavy handed in my opinions on code design and quality since that work would become part of the external API of FilterPy. For tests I just ask that the code be reasonably PEP8 compliant, with NumPy style docstrings and coding conventions.

> I'm not sure why in write_stream in better-exceptions code, the data is first encoded and then written to buffer, instead of leaving the data unencoded and just using write. That seems to work.

This is because this could result in encoding errors (if for example the string being sent contains `utf-8` characters while the `sys.stderr` default configuration uses `ascii`).
See https://github.com/Qix-/better-exceptions/pull/9 and https://github.com/Qix-/better-exceptions/pull/33

I fixed the error by wrapping the stream wrapped by Colorama. So, when Colorama uses `.write(substring)` on my stream for substrings between two win32 commands, my wrapping stream can encode and send the bytes string to the wrapped stream buffer. :sweat_smile:
See https://github.com/Qix-/better-exceptions/pull/42.

Anyway, thanks for the information about the `buffer` not being wrapped, that helped me.

Thumbs up to remove precision completely from the type declaration since the OFX spec doesn't have a concept of precision either.

As for `</SONRQ>`. Basically Vanguard returns a 400 response in their OFX endpoint unless you place a newline right before `</SONRQ>`. I discovered this after noticing that an OFX request with newlines liberally placed worked, and without newlines didn't work (for the same exact request body). I experimented by placing a single newline in the single-line response at various valid locations until it worked, and that was the spot that made it work. I think that is best place to put that hacked workaround but you may a prefer a different location. Don't feel obligated to merge that specific change but it may help other people who deal with Vanguard. I use these settings for Vanguard, maybe there is a better less-buggy endpoint:

    org = "Vanguard"
    fid = None
    brokerid = "vanguard.com"
    version = 102
    url = "https://vesnc.vanguard.com/us/OfxDirectConnectServlet
    client = OFXClient(url, org=org, fid=fid, brokerid=brokerid, version=version)

Dear @splitbrain, thanks for you report. I've never used any of the QL series printers with a print server. So it's a good use case to test against.

Concerning the timeout: the documentation of socket.sendall() [says](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket.socket.sendall) the behaviour with respect to `timeout` changed with Python 3.5:

> Changed in version 3.5: The socket timeout is no more reset each time data is sent successfully. The socket timeout is now the maximum total duration to send all data.

I think we should raise the timeout to a higher value as you suggest, to block in case of back-pressure on the socket.

So here is what I'm thinking, as always I'd appreciate any thoughts and feedback. Primarily I want the API to be as simple as possible for users, and also straightforward to implement for each backend.

I'm in favor of extending the `Bus` directly with a `send_periodic` method. This method could either take a `CyclicSendTask` argument or, a `Message` and period information. I'm not quite convinced either way yet so I'd like to discuss the pros and cons...

Using a `CyclicTask` object:
- (-) More complexity for users. Most users probably just want the most basic of periodic sending to work really well and I suspect an api like `bus.send_periodic(msg, period, repeats)` might cater to most use cases.
- (+) More flexibility for users. We could use the same Bus method to pass a few different types of tasks (e.g `MultiRateCyclicSendTask` or more complex instructions). We could have a library dispatcher and default pure python implementations and leave it up to each interface to override pieces.
- (+) Might be more explicit because the user would have created a "Task" object which could later be used to stop and modify the task. As opposed to sending a periodic message and having to keep the returned `PeriodicTask`.
- What else?

This public api Bus level method would probably return an object like `PeriodicTask` which could be used to modify or cancel it later. This has the potential for introducing threading issues so would have to be designed with something like queues or events to prevent them.

We can implement a concrete default implementation of this entire idea in the Bus's abstract base class and each interface would be encouraged to pass off to the hardware where it makes the most sense for them. IMO The tricky part is working out the api.

The same test is failing here with Python 3.5 on an x86_64 Ubuntu 16.04 box. It doesn't always fail - there's a timing issue - but it does most of the time.

I've had a look at it, and I can say the following is happening:

- `IMAP4.wait_server_push` returns the last thing present on `IMAP4ClientProtocol.idle_queue`
- The test in question expects that value to be `STOP_WAIT_SERVER_PUSH`, which is added on the queue by `IMAP4.stop_wait_server_push`, which is called 29 minutes (the default) after `IMAP4.idle_start` was invoked. The purpose of this is that, even though we're waiting for server to push things to us, we should talk to it every 29 minutes as it might time us out otherwise;
- What happens when the test fails is that `IdleCommand.flush` is invoked **after** `IMAP4.stop_wait_server_push`, and that adds on top of `IMAP4ClientProtocal.idle_queue` a number of 'OK Still here' messages that were received from the server and buffered on `IdleCommand.buffer`. So the last entry on that queue is not `STOP_WAIT_SERVER_PUSH` as the test was expecting.

I don't know yet whether it is expected that `IdleCommand.flush` can be invoked after `IMAP4.stop_wait_server_push`. Depending on that, there is either a problem in the code, or a problem in the test.

I won't have time to look at it more for a few days, so if anyone has some further insights feel free to continue working on this one :-)

I can see where you're coming from, but [the documentation says](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/en/latest/worksheet.html#write) that calling `str()` is the intended behaviour (as well as `float()`), which is why I filed a bug report rather than just working around it.

> The rules for handling data in write() are as follows:

> ...

> If none of the above types are matched the value is evaluated with float() to see if it corresponds to a user defined float type. If it does then it is written using write_number().

> If not then it is evaluated with str() to see if it corresponds to a user defined string type. If it does then it is written using write_string().

> Finally, if none of these rules are matched then a TypeError exception is raised.

Plus, the call to `str()` is actually there in [`worksheet.py` lines 430-435](https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/blob/master/xlsxwriter/worksheet.py#L430-L435), it just never gets that far because the preceding lines raise `TypeError` when `float()` fails.

Just verified that the PSRML is optional in Sikuli (despite the documentation to the contrary).

It's not quite as simple as making it an optional argument in Python, to preserve compatibility, as we have to support the following argument lists:

    (int direction, int steps)
    (PFRML target, int direction, int steps)
    (PFRML target, int direction, int steps, int stepDelay)

This just means we have to figure out which pattern of parameters have been passed. (There are a few other methods like this as well).

My toy experiment
```
define type
    real
    float is a real
    double is a real
```
proved to me that trying to shoehorn a line-based language in a CFG parser is not feasible. It's too bad, as I could really use a parser for the structure inside a line (not today, but likely tomorrow).

I think the whole problem starts with the parsing theory seeing input as a contiguous stream of bytes/characters, while in reality, humans think and work in lines of text. Your grammar specification already proves it. Most grammar lines fit on a single line, the terminating `;` is just clutter. If you defined the LHS to be at the left margin, and everything else not at the left margin, that would work nicer.

My experiment above runs above LR(1) parsing capabilities, while in a line-based setting it would be mostly trivial something like
```
typesection : typeheader_line definetype_line+
typeheader_line : "define" "type"
definetype_line : NAMETK | NAMETK "is" "a" NAMETK
```
where `*_line` nonterminals only match at full lines, and I conveniently consider an empty line to be white space.

However, I don't know of any tool that allows me to write this in a convenient way.

In a line-based setting, you quickly end up in 'sections' of text. I have `define type` as header to denote a type section, column/indentation is another form of creating a section, I think.

This is not fixable, at least not easily.

You would need to add some kind of `set_all` method and also a way to distinguish traversal results from ordinary ones. If I understand everything correctly, both of these would require inelegant hacks. I would rather keep the limitation and just improve documentation.

On the one hand, it seems like an inadvertent side-effect of the implementation. `cached_property` is currently a [non-data descriptor](https://docs.python.org/2/howto/descriptor.html) and as such it may be overridden by instance dictionaries. If a `__set__` method is added, it becomes a data descriptor and data descriptors always override instance dictionaries. Still, it is possible to change the implementation so that it works correctly even as data descriptor like the latest [`cached_property_with_ttl`](https://github.com/pydanny/cached-property/blob/master/cached_property.py#L56).

On the other hand, one could argue that being settable is more of a feature than a bug. I think the major characteristic of cached properties is that they don't incur a (potentially expensive) computation *once the value of the property is available*. Whether the value became available implicitly through an initial `get` operation or explicitly through a `set` is not relevant.

>
> The |upload| method on Redmine object just uploads a file to the 
> Redmine and returns an assigned token that identifies this file inside 
> Redmine. Then you can use this token to attach this uploaded file to 
> the resource that supports attachments, for example Issue or Wikipage. 
> If you just upload a file and don't attach it to the resource, it will 
> just stay in Redmine, if you lose a token, you can't get it again 
> through the REST API, you need to search for it manually inside the 
> Redmine database.
>
> That's all that you can do with it for now. And no, it's not possible 
> to upload a file via |upload| and fetch it's url, BUT if you upload 
> and then attach it to some Resource, it becomes an Attachment resource 
> by itself (surprise, surprise), and then it will have an id, url and 
> so on, and you can use |redmine.attachment.get(ID)| to get this info 
> from it.
>
> The low-level details of this process are described here 
> <http://www.redmine.org/projects/redmine/wiki/Rest_api#Attaching-files>.

Hi Leo,

You only need to run `mvn package` if you want to use the [Spark Scala Inferencing API](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/wiki/Scala-Inference-API).  Otherwise, the rest of TFoS is pure python, so you can either `pip install tensorflowonspark` on your Spark nodes.  Or, if you don't have privilege to do that, you can ship it to the executors via `--pyfiles tfspark.zip`. Okay, finally got a chance to dig into this.

Using the Money resource is the correct way to do it as you mentioned you read in the tests:
https://github.com/recurly/recurly-client-python/blob/master/tests/test_resources.py#L154

The way the python library works with serialization is that it maps a python level 'Resource' to an xml level tag. The resulting xml here for `unit_amount_in_cents` is:

```xml
<unit_amount_in_cents>
  <USD>200</USD>
</unit_amount_in_cents>
```

and using the Money resource allows changing the currency, etc. It's a strange/inconsistent part of the API because usually it's an integer and the currency is specified in another field such as transactions#create:

```xml
<amount_in_cents type="integer">1000</amount_in_cents>
<tax_in_cents type="integer">0</tax_in_cents>
<currency>USD</currency>
```

or invoice#post

```xml
<subtotal_in_cents type="integer">1200</subtotal_in_cents>
<tax_in_cents type="integer">0</tax_in_cents>
<total_in_cents type="integer">1200</total_in_cents>
<currency>USD</currency>
```

This is further complicated by the fact that the schema for add_ons in the subscription schema also follows this pattern:

```xml
<subscription_add_ons type="array">
  <subscription_add_on>
    <add_on_code>ipaddresses</add_on_code>
    <quantity>10</quantity>
    <unit_amount_in_cents>150</unit_amount_in_cents>
  </subscription_add_on>
</subscription_add_ons>
```

I want to say the best way forward would be to change the xml schema for add_ons so that it supports the method used everywhere else and deprecates the old method but it seems to be used in other places. Let me discuss with the team and report back here.

Well, about tox integration there's no problem there (tox was created by pytest's creator, Holger Krekel). :smile:

Test discovery speed should be about the same in nose and pytest, but if your concern is about running the test suite during development, pytest also has some excellent filtering options that you can use in the command line (for example, `py.test -k datetime` will run only tests with have `"datetime"` on module, file or test name). Plus there's a lot of plugins to try out:

* [pytest-stepwise](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pytest-stepwise) allows to run the test suite until the first test fails; when executing again, start from the last failed test and onward until a new test fails, and so on until the whole suite finishes. 
* [pytest-instafail](https://github.com/jpvanhal/pytest-instafail) allows to display failure tracebacks while the test suite is running, instead of only at the end.

This is just a few examples from the top of my head.

Also, you can configure pytest to do not descend into certain directories via a configuration file, so that's not a problem.

> Maybe pytest can replace both nose and the hand-written tests/runtests.py in this workflow?

Sure! 

> I'm especially interested if it can make the test runs faster. 

If the problem is test run time, we can try distributed testing (`nose` has support for it too); about test collection we will have to see.

pytest has a lot more to offer too, but don't worry, the objective of this movement is exactly to bring awareness to people who haven't look into pytest yet. :smile:

That's the object i printed above - it's not any kind of object understood by FBX, just something with the name "ProxyObject_Mauerwerk - Ziegel" and a hashed reference ID "A3A549DA-616A-4977-B74F-AFED98A871FA".

Presumably the reference will be understood by Revit, using its own internal data handling system. But sadly there's no way for another program to understand what it means, or use it in any meaningful way.

If it were an FBX material, it would be defined as a "Material", not an "Object", and assimp would indeed add it to the list of materials.

The best option if you want to analyze the FBX and extract data like this would probably be to convert it to JSON and use a JSON parsing tool, or else to use the FBX SDK which should have some way of iterating over all the available objects.

OK.  I finally found the problem.  In Assimp 3.3.1, IrrXML is builtin, and now Assimp.xcodeproj builds libIrrXml.a as well as libassimp.a.  I wasn't aware of the change.

I tried searching the documentation, and I found this in the CHANGE notes:

    `Apply mechanism to decide use for IrrXML external or internal`

I also found this in the CMakeLists.txt:

```
# Compile internal irrXML only if system is not requested
if( NOT SYSTEM_IRRXML )
    add_subdirectory(irrXML)
endif( NOT SYSTEM_IRRXML )
```

I think it would help to add the details about this to the INSTALL notes.

```py
import boto3
from boto3.session import Config

import boto3
s3 = boto3.client(
    's3', endpoint_url='https://foo.com',
    config=Config(s3={'addressing_style': 'virtual'})
)

s3.list_objects(Bucket='mybucket')
```
That will use the virtual style of addressing. If you need the path style of addressing, just specify ``"path"`` instead.

Generally speaking, `resolver.py` is invoked _inside_ the context of the virtualenv we resolve dependencies against.  So roughly speaking:

```
$ pipenv install <somepackage> 
=> calls site-packages/lib/pipenv/cli.py
==> ensures virtualenv in $WORKON_HOME/$(cwd)-$(deterministic_hash) or $(cwd)/.venv
====> generates lockfile by calling $virtualenv/bin/python $global_pipenv_dir/resolver.py <whatever>
```

You can see the last step is in the context of a new virtualenv, which doesn't have the originating environment's python path anymore which is why debundling things loses them.  You'll find in `cli.py` and in other parts of our code we specifically insert pipenv onto `sys.path` at runtime to account for this

> setup(install_requires=["autobahn[twisted] >= 0.14.1"])
> is equivalent to
> setup(extras_require={'twisted': ["autobahn >= 0.14.1"]})

I'm pretty sure this statement is wrong.

The former syntax says "this package depends on autobahn with its twisted extra and autobahn should be version 0.14.1 or later". The latter syntax says "this package offers an optional feature called "twisted" and if you ask for thispackage[twisted], this package will additionally depend on autobahn 0.14.1 or later."

@pganssle 

> I don't know if I understand the meaning of "externally" in this context. Under what conditions would long_description_content_type be set other than through a call to setup?

So a PBR based installer uses simply

```python
setuptools.setup(
    setup_requires=['pbr>=2.0.0'],
    pbr=True)
```

The `pbr` argument basically ends up in https://git.openstack.org/cgit/openstack-dev/pbr/tree/pbr/core.py ; which reads a `setup.cfg` and you can see how it populates various fields of the `Distribute` object from that.

This works for updating all the original distutils-based metadata settings, etc.  But `long_description_content_type` is different in that setuptools unconditionally sets it *after* the PBR setup() keyword handler has run.  Hence this change to allow the value to populate there first and not be overwritten.

I'm open to ideas on what testing you'd like to see.  The tests as is ensure that this didn't regress and not set the value from `attr`.

To get IRAF's `merr` nearly exactly, you actually need to do local background computation, with local background error estimation as well.  This is tricky using PhotUtils alone, but I've actually implemented it rather recently (though it seems @larrybradley also did it very recently [here](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-workshop/blob/master/09-Photutils/photutils_local_backgrounds.ipynb).  Anyway, I've simply just taken it a step further and computed the DAOPHOT style errors by implementing the same formula in Python.  

The code lives over at https://github.com/spacetelescope/wfc3_photometry/blob/master/photometry_tools/photometry_tools.py.  An example is in the docstring, but for your posted code I think it should be something like:

```python
from photometry_tools import iraf_style_photometry

photometry_tbl = iraf_style_photometry(
        apertures, annulus_apertures, hdu_data,
        error_array=None, bg_method='mean') # median and mode are also valid options
```

This should get you errors more in line with what IRAF would produce. There are a few small caveats, but generally it seems the magnitudes agree to ~0.01 mags on average (1%) and the errors agree to ~.001 mag (the fainter stars are worse, though I think this is more an IRAF limitation).

I'm usually bullish on numba, but I recently saw that you can't "pip install datashader" because they are concerned about missing dependencies. From the [README](https://github.com/bokeh/datashader/):

> Datashader is not currently available on PyPI, to avoid broken or low-performance installations that come from not keeping track of C/C++ binary dependencies such as LLVM (required by Numba).

I would be reluctant on taking on this dependency until such concerns are addressed.

As regards performance, my experience has been that Numba is marginally faster than comparable code, so I would be inclined to trust your benchmarks. A big upside you didn't mention, though, is that for debugging you can just do `NUMBA_DISABLE_JIT=1` and then use pdb/ipdb in all its glory.

We might as well use this PR to discuss a roadmap for performant code in the future. I think we might actually be able to implement an optional dependency, where the Numba version is used if available, or the Cython version if it isn't, using the new Pure Python mode in Cython:

http://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/pure.html

This is related to mysql's wishy-washy position on the NO_ZERO_DATE flag that has gone and come again starting with 5.7.4. See "SQL Mode Changes in MySQL 5.7" http://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/sql-mode.html#sql-mode-changes. Apparently, as of mysql 5.7.8, NO_ZERO_DATE is back in the default mode set. Apparently, you can get rid of the mentioned error by removing NO_ZERO_DATE from mysql's config file (my.ini?). According to this note, "The long term plan is still to have the three affected modes be included in strict SQL mode and to remove them as explicit modes in a future MySQL release. ", but they keep going back and forth to give affected apps additional time to modify their schema.

I think that we need to modify the affected schema to use datetime column attributes that are compatible with mysql 5.6 and 5.7.8+ and don't break the application logic (or update affected logic, if needed), in case something in the logic relies on the original default value.

Vitaly

Just recording decisions taking IRL:

- We are deprecating DynamicPermission in favour of Permission. DynamicPermission will allow by default if an action has not been assigned, this has now been reverted to align with Flask-Principel to by default deny if an action has not been assigned.
- We did not make any changes to superuser-access.
- We've added system roles (such as any user/authenticated user)

Because of above change, we think it works logically now:
- By default an action is restricted for any parameter.
- If user A is granted action with parameter 5 then that works.
- If user B is granted superuser-access he can see the action with any parameter.

In order to achieve that all records are visible, except record 5 you would have to:
1. Allow the action with any parameter to system role any user. 
2. Deny the action with parameter 5 to system role any user.
3. Allow the action with parameter 5 to user A

i object auto-correcting such things as long term it will introduce more errors than it solves.

Let's extend your example a bit...:
```
https://allevents.in/santa rita
https://allevents.in/santa%20rita?query= foo bar&bla  # only query part unescaped? is the & part of the query value or a new param?
https://allevents.in/santa+rita?query= foo bar  # other common " " replacement, should query part do it similar?
https://allevents.in/santa_rita?query= foo bar  # wikipedia " " replacement
http://example.com/jörn  # did you actually mean the IRI (UTF-8 'ö') or URI ('%C3%B6')?
...
```

I see that it is tempting to say "auto-correct the simple and extremely common stuff". However, we have to weigh this against providing a consistent, deterministic lib. I'm quite convinced that the way we handle this, namely expect the developer to give us correct URIs is the least problematic in the end.

Given that: warnings in early development are a good way to make a developer aware. If some developer uses invalid URIs as URIs, then they should definitely know about this. In production code without a configured logger that warning isn't shown if i'm not mistaken. The other cases are:
- you have a logger configured (then please configure it as you like, the rdflib logging messages are in the rdflib namespace, if you want to silence them, do so)
- if you don't have a logger configured:
  - if you're in interactive mode, then you're probably developing and should know
  - if you're not, then the warnings aren't shown

The issue is that the brighter-fatter effect cares which direction the parallel and serial transfer directions are in that the deposited charge is redistributed more easily along the parallel transfer direction, and this is conventionally taken to be the y-direction.  However, the CCS has the parallel transfers along in the x-axis, and the sims code returns object positions and orientations in the CCS.  The galsim code treats the BF effect as if the parallel transfers are in the y-direction, so in order to get the BF anisotropy correct we either have to tell galsim to transpose the x and y axes for the BF calculations or have the sims code provide object positions and orientations in a transposed version of the CCS.

Mike - I just uploaded the new I<25.2 catalog to my CMU Box account, so I can share it with you (you should get a separate e-mail in a few minutes).  I was wondering if you would mind playing with it before we put it on the GREAT3 server and advertise it more widely from the GalSim wiki?  To facilitate this, I made a new ```cgc_faint.yaml``` that works with the actual faint sample instead of faking it from the I<23.5 sample.  It is already pushed to this branch.  I confirmed that it runs and the output doesn't look ridiculous, but would appreciate any comments you have either by running this or just playing with the data.

A few notes and questions:
- If you load up a COSMOSCatalog based on this fainter sample, you will notice that the "marginal" cuts exclude a higher fraction of galaxies than for the I<23.5 sample.  That's because those cuts were really calibrated for the 23.5 sample, and would need to change (e.g., they include a ratio of average flux in masked pixels near the object compared to the peak flux of the object, the latter of which should be different for a fainter sample).  So for now the new script doesn't use the default "marginal" cut but rather uses the "bad_fits" cut.  I am trying to come up with a work-around.  The most obvious one I can think of requires yet another change to the ```blah_selection.fits``` files: instead of including the quantities that go into the mask cuts, so that the COSMOSCatalog class can impose the cuts itself, I could do the mask cuts separately (with different cut values) for each sample and just include a flag called "masked" in the ```blah_selection.fits``` for use by COSMOSCatalog.

Of course I only thought of this now, after re-making the tarballs many times.  It figures.  One mor time can't hurt, right?  Anyway, this is the best idea I have, so unless you have a better one, I think I will do this.

- I thought of one potential problem (I think?) that makes cgc.yaml etc. not quite comparable to GREAT3: correct me if I'm wrong, but unlike in GREAT3, I think the postage stamp-level rejection is done in a way that could result in us chucking out one part of a 90-degree rotated pair?

@jkrukoff there's a way to do this yes. First you need to indicate that the test should be skipped in python versions that do not support enums. You can use the [@unittest.skipIf][1] decorator:

```python
@unittest.skipIf(sys.version_info < (3, 5), "Not supported in this version")
def test_your_test_name(...):
```

If you want to run your tests with multiple python versions, we're using [tox][2] for this. To verify that the above works you can run tox with `-e` command line parameter as follows:

```bash
$ tox -e py27,py34,py36  
```

You can install tox with `pip`:

```bash
$ pip install tox
```

@mragaei Yup - its sounds insane, but the domain name behavior is NTLMv2 alright.

NTLMv2 re-purposes and extends NTLMv1 to retain necessary compatibility. The Negotiate Type1 is pretty much the same for both protocols. The client still sends its domain name in the Type 1 structure - however, in NTLMv2, its is _ignored_. Instead the server responds with its Domain Name in the TargetInfo structure in the Type 2 message - and it is this domain name which forms part of the raw material for the hash.
Also, ordering of the domain name and computer name is also not important as long as the offset and length of the names are correctly set following the header. 

I'm sure there is an issue here, but it's probably very subtle. I really need to take a look at the raw message exchange between ntlmlib and the server in question. Are you able to email me a packet capture (tcpdump or wireshark)?

The code changes in this PR break many different integration tests which explicitly assume that the results directory for a task will just be named the `stable_name()` of the `Task` synthesized subclass (e.g. `ZincCompile.stable_name()`). With the code changes in this PR, you now can't know the name of the results directory for a task until it is instantiated, which means until after running the pants subprocess and examining the cache directory again for many integration tests.

I've been successfully converting multiple integration tests to reflect the new behavior today, but I think I can make this PR much smaller if I instead move the duplicated logic into a method on the `PantsRunIntegrationTest` class and call that. I'll look into doing that so I don't have to keep changing the body of every single failing test method (this will also make changing the way we fingerprint tasks in the future much easier, if we ever want to do that for some reason).

I also had a good discussion with @baroquebobcat about making the test cases I added to `test_task.py` more readable, so I will attempt to do that in between waiting for integration tests to run.

Trying to write tests for this by leveraging the internal interfaces will be quite challenging. 
There's a vt100 terminal emulator called [pyte](https://github.com/selectel/pyte) that we could leverage to have the output of the shell go to an in memory string representation of the current terminal screen. Though, that only solves one problem and would likely also require a custom event loop that would allow us to provide input, inspect the current screen state, then provide more input, etc. I think it would be quite an effort to wire the prompt toolkit internals up in this way.

I also found [hecate](https://github.com/DRMacIver/hecate) which is a testing framework designed for testing ncurses like terminal applications. It leverages tmux as a terminal emulator and interfaces with a tmux session to send input and determine the current screen state. It's relatively simple to write tests with it and I have one in this PR to test these changes. Unfortunately, actually running an instance of the shell to run tests against it currently is being blocked by having the documentation generate in the background. It would be better to have all the documentation already generated then run these kind of integration tests against them for consistency.

The copyfrom stuff is needed when `$` is applied to a string variable.  I think it needs to copy all the variables in the program over, so that the temporary state has them.

Currently the machinery for this is duplicated in each backend which needs it, despite it being pretty much the same for everything except C (where we just do a `struct` copy).  The base class copies the members it holds, but can't copy members which are in the subclass.  The only slight trick is that is `copyfrom()` isn't needed for a particular snowball program, we avoid generating `copyfrom()` for the corresponding subclass.

I am attempting to factor out common code like this, as repeating so much code is dumb for hopefully obvious reasons.  But that means if it's not working, it's presumably down to the code in your backend!

> "com.schriftgestaltung.customParameter.InstanceDescriptorAsGSInstance.Keep Glyphs"

Yes, this is a stupidly long name. I'm going to explain how that came to be, so that a better solution can be found:
* for font & master custom parameters, I added "GSFont" or "GSFontMaster" inside the name, because both font and master custom parameters end up in the same UFO lib. When we go in the other direction, we can know from the name where the custom parameter should go.
    * this is happening here as well for instance custom parameters ("InstanceDescriptorAsGSInstance"), but it is unnecessary because those custom parameters are not meant to "go back" to glyphs, since they are added to a UFO that has been freshly interpolated by fontmake/mutatorMath and that will be discarded when we get a TTF, and also because if this customParameter from the GSInstance is in conflict with one that came e.g. from the GSFont, then the instance value should overwrite (or be appended?) to the value from the GSFont.
    * even in the case of GSFont vs GSFontMaster, we could do:
        * GSFont <-> designspace lib
        * GSFontMaster <-> UFO lib
        * GSInstance <-> designspace instance lib
    and thus there is a one-to-one mapping and no need for that part of the key name.

    This part is added here:
https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/b35f4abe16df4d49f687833dbf841b9d7b0ec281/Lib/glyphsLib/builder/custom_params.py#L77-L79

* the key starts by "com.schriftgestaltung.customParameter" because I wanted to be sure of what is a custom parameter and what is not. A key like "com.github.googlei18n.glyphsLib.custom" would be fine as well. The key prefix is here:
https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/b35f4abe16df4d49f687833dbf841b9d7b0ec281/Lib/glyphsLib/builder/custom_params.py#L66

@JelleBosmaMT : FYI

for parallelizing python programs that do CPU intensive stuff (non I/O intensive) one cannot use multi-threading because of the infamous CPython's global interpreter lock (GIL). So one has to use multiprocessing. But unlike threads, sub-processes by default don't share memory with the parent process and communication between them may require serialization and deserialization (read `pickle`); plus, on platforms that don't have `fork` like windows spawning new process is relatively expensive.
But yes, we definitely need to look into that at some point.

I think the discussion is heading towards consensus (inheritance = bad, observers = good, ..). One thing though is to define/document the events that can be observed on a session.

Eg., currently we have these https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/component.py#L398 events:

1. **start**
2. **connect**: fired when a transport is connected _to_ the session (the transport itself must be itself "connected" at the network level to be of use to the session of course)
3. **join**: fired when the WAMP opening handshake is completed (HELLO -> CHALLENGE -> AUTHENTICATE -> WELCOME)
4. **ready**: fired when ALL observers on "join" have finished
5. **leave**
6. **disconnect**: wired when the transport is disconnected _from_ the session (the transport itself might stay "connected" at the network level to be reused with the same or a different session)

---

Deis utilizes kubernetes under the hood for v2 which currently does not have full rkt support. In terms of how difficult it would be to support the two (Docker and rkt), here's a list of things I can come up with:

 - need an internal registry to store rkt ACIs from application builds
 - rkt support in kubernetes for deploying across the cluster (somewhat available but not out-of-the-box, and isn't ready for production)
 - rkt support in [deis/builder](https://github.com/deis/builder) for `git push deis master` to build a rkt ACI
 - `deis pull` support for rkt ACIs

The difficulty of pulling off such a feature would be very high, as it affects and touches nearly all points in Deis and would require intimate knowledge of the platform. I'm not trying to discourage such an idea, but it would be a lot of time.

Keep in mind that this is just the application workflow as well. If we wanted Deis to be deployable using rkt, we would then need to make the travis builds punch out ACIs and kubernetes manifest files, which would also mean either waiting for TravisCI to have rkt support or to use another CI provider in conjunction with Travis. We'd also need our new installer [helm](http://helm.sh/) to have knowledge about deploying a rkt implementation of Deis onto kubernetes.

Hope this helps :)

    conda config --set channel_priority false

but that can then lead to other problems and issues.

In conda 4.4, you can specify exact channels you want packages to come from.  For example

    conda install defaults::scikit-bio

will keep the `scikit-bio` pinned to the `defaults` channel until you drop the specification with something like `conda update scikit-bio` or `conda update --all`.

A missing `hfile_plugin_init_libcurl()` would mean that _plugin.c_ was compiled with `HAVE_LIBCURL` set and `ENABLE_PLUGINS` not set, but _hfile_libcurl.o_ did not make it into the library.

If this is limited to OS X, it is likely because the [_pysam: libcurl has not been found - disabled_ test](https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/master/setup.py#L290-L292) does not account for `HAVE_COMMONCRYPTO`.  This might be a demonstration of how misguided this sort of code is, but that would be a digression :smile:

It seems I could fool this logic by artificially setting the `name` attribute in the passed Python fileobject. Given `"SampleX.bam"` starting with alignments on `Chr.1` and a fake file called `"foo"`. `samtools view` would be used for filtering alignments, but in this example it was used to restrict output to only those alignments which where mapped in `Chr.5`.

```python

import os
import subprocess

import pysam

fake = "foo" 

# Create an empty file to fool filelookup
os.system("touch %s" % fake)

class MyIOWrapper(io.TextIOWrapper):
    @property
    def name(self):
        return fake

with subprocess.Popen(["samtools", "view", "-u", "SampleX.bam", "Chr.5"], stdout=subprocess.PIPE) as proc:
    fh = MyIOWrapper(proc.stdout)
    with pysam.AlignmentFile(fh, "rb"") as sam:
        for rec in sam:
            print(rec.reference_name)
            break
```

Prints out: `Chr.5`, indicating it was reading the output from the sub-process. For clearance, the `name` in `MyIOWrapper` could be set to `"SampleX.bam"` as well, as long as that file exists!

Hi Christian,

The CI/CD pipeline creation commands in the Azure CLI and ACS portal blade have been deprecated. We experimented with the ‘az container release create’ functionality, but based on user feedback and product usage, it became clear that we needed to improve container experiences directly within the VS Team Services portal and client tools like Visual Studio IDE, as well as target a broader set of deployment environments.  At //build 2017, experience improvements were made available for Visual Studio and Team Services to support container CI/CD to App Services and Service Fabric, with Kubernetes coming soon. Note that any CI/CD pipelines that were originally created with the ‘az container release create’ command will continue to function properly, because it created VSTS build/release definitions and Azure resources like Azure Container Service and Azure Container Registry that are supported products.

More information:
[Continuous Delivery Tools for Visual Studio](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=VSIDEDevOpsMSFT.ContinuousDeliveryToolsforVisualStudio)

Regards,
John

I think they should not be moved to single requests but to **ClientSession** instead. Multiple requests to the same host+port can reuse connection (or also not reuse, it's statically unknown in general) — https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/3da3a0142e3f8a50c990824ea6d4669643a9fa9e/aiohttp/connector.py#L340 and TLS params are inherently property of the connection, not a HTTP request, so if the user gives different TLS parameters to these requests, that would be very misleading and even non-stable behavior (because it's not known in general if the connection will be reused for a specific request).

Ok folks, I now have a shell script available for building a static cryptography wheel on Linux against OpenSSL 1.0.2. The script is currently in pyca/cryptography#2338: keep an eye on that issue, because when it's merged we should start pointing at the cryptography documentation.

Please note that to build the wheel you will need to install a C compiler, the Python headers, and libffi. On ubuntu that's `apt-get install build-essential python-dev libffi-dev`. On Red Hat/Fedora, that's `yum install gcc python-devel libffi-devel`. You should then be able to run the shell script and end up with a directory called `wheelhouse` that contains wheels of cryptography and its relevant dependencies. Please also note that you need to build this wheel on something that looks *a lot* like your target environment.

You can then install these wheels (or just the cryptography wheel, if you want) to a virtualenv on your target box. To do that you will need a relatively recent `pip` and the `wheel` module: the one that comes with your system may not be good enough (it isn't on Ubuntu 12.04). It should be totally safe to update `pip` and `wheel` in a virtualenv though, so if you're using one on your deploy boxes I recommend doing a `pip install -U pip wheel` before you begin. It should then be a simple case of `pip install cryptography<whatever>.whl`. You should then have OpenSSL 1.0.2 goodness in your deployment environment, no compiler required.

I'm trying to summarize the options mentioned above:

1. `pip install ...`: should work on all Python3-only systems (including `venv`s), but will likely install the wrong thing on mixed Python2/3 systems.

1. `pip3 install ...`: should work on many systems, but not on Anaconda. Python2 users just have to drop the "3".

1. `python3 -m pip install ...`: should work on all systems. Python2 users just have to drop the "3".

Did I miss anything?

The more I think about it, the more I like the last option. It looks a bit awkward at first, but I think I can get over that.

It has several advantages:

* It is a clear statement that Python 3 should be used
* It is easy to experiment with different Python version, e.g. `python3.4` vs `python3.5`
* Many commands in the docs will look more consistent when using `python3 -m pip ...` and `python3 setup.py ...`

The same thing could also be used for `python3 -m venv`, but this'll have to be changed to `python -m virtualenv` for Python 2.

Those assertion could valid if the keys of the dictionary are valid BibTeX's field and that the value associated to each key is of ```str``` type. Overall, I stick to my guns thinking the simplest way to adapt the package for this issue would be allow the passage of a hand made dictionary to the constructor of the ```BibTexParser``` class. The only challenge with "automatically put the dict in a list when there were multiples" is that it defeats the purpose for the generators.  Think about times when you are "listing" things...

**Rooms:**  If you have a lot of rooms and you are listing the rooms to find a specific one (which usually is a recent one), it can take multiple / many API calls to request 'all' of the rooms.  And, if the room is let's say in the first 10 or 20 rooms, all of the following API calls weren't needed.

Messages:  What if you requested "the list" of messages for a decently trafficed room that has existed for let's say over a year.  That room could contain a lot of messages.  If you are only interested in the recent messages, there would be a ton of wasted API calls (and memory to store all of the objects).

The generators, which incrementally yield the results (requesting additional pages only as needed) are more efficient.  If you find what you need, you can stop the iteration and not make any more requests than are needed.  If you want all of the items, you can of course continue the iteration until all items have been returned - in essence you are in the drivers seat.  😎

Plus, if you really want a list (which will contain all the requested items), you can always just pass the generator container to the `list()` constructor which will build a list from all of the generated items.

``` python
rooms_generator = api.rooms.list()
rooms_list = list(rooms_generator)
```

`self.idf[k] = math.log(0.5) - math.log( v + 0.5)` whereas `v` is a natural value (integer > 0).

> How about the case when we need to select option from choices?

Then those need to be stored as `Option`s on the product. In cases where attributes are identical to options, you could auto-generate the `Option`s based on the attributes, but this would be up to the implementor. From Oscar's perspective attributes are a generic way to store *any* information about a product.

The error indicates what the issue is - something on your machine/network is blocking network requests. It's not possible to say what request is being blocked without seeing the full stack trace - my best guess is upon registration Oscar is trying to send a registration email and you don't have an SMTP server set up, so this fails.

Also please note this warning [from the documentation](http://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/releases-1.6/internals/sandbox.html#run-the-sandbox-locally): 

> While installing Oscar is straightforward, some of Oscar’s dependencies don’t support Windows and are tricky to be properly installed, and therefore you might encounter some errors that prevent a successful installation.

I'd go further and say that you will definitely not get a production instance of Oscar working on Windows. It's also not straightforward to get a database server set up on Windows.

Closing this as it's not a bug in Oscar, but an issue with your local environment. If you need further help please use the support channels - the mailing list or Stack Overflow.

I wonder if there should be 3 options for offset config:
 * earliest (reset offset and start from beginning [start over])
 * resume (last offset for group if exists, otherwise beginning [where we left off])
 * latest (start and last index for topic [now onwards])

@kevgliss I agree with @deckar01 that you can access context via `self` if you make validation a schema's member function. Although IMO this kind of validations should not be done during deserialization. Instead you check them afterwards on deserialized data. After giving this some thought, I think `ContainsOnly` should accept empty values as valid inputs. Validating against empty vs. non-empty is outside the responsibility of `ContainsOnly`. If you need to validate that input is non-empty, use `Length(min=1)`.

If possible, we should have the same structure as what the `List` field generates, except it would have "keys" rather than "indexes".

From `List`:

```python
errors.update({idx: e.messages})
```

Maybe `errors[key]` should be a `dict` rather than a `list`, with at most two keys: `key` for the list of key errors and `value` for the list of value errors.

```python
errors[key] = {
    'key': [],
    'value': [],
}
```

We could do (untested):

```python
                    key = keys[idx]
                    if key not in errors:  # Avoid this test by using a defaultdict?
                        errors[key] = {}
                    errors[key]['value'] = e.messages
```

and likewise for key errors.

To be closer to `List`, `errors` itself could be a `list` rather than a `dict`. But I don't see any benefit to this apart from having similar structures for both. And it makes sense that `List` has an error `list` and `Dict` an error `dict`.

There are various max_* options in Z3, but I don't think any of them would be a solution to your problem. Could you elaborate on why timeouts are not good enough for you? 

As long as the default Z3 tactics are used, there will always be an element of non-determinism, because some of them use timers on pre-processors, so sometimes it will end up with a whole different simplification of the problem, such that any other counter on conflicts or similar things will still be non-deterministic. (see e.g., all the try_for's in the QF_LIA default tactic: https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/smtlogics/qflia_tactic.cpp). In some cases the SMT kernel is also not used at all, because some other part of the tactical framework will solve the problem without ever counting any conflicts, but still requiring non-deterministic, long runtime. I don't think there is a simple solution to this problem that's better than using time/memouts.

Hi, digging this up since I've stumbled upon the same problem.
I believe the issue is in the [implementation of `__libc_start_main`](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/procedures/glibc/__libc_start_main.py#L136), where it says:

```python
        if isinstance(self.state.arch, ArchAMD64):
            # (rsp+8) must be aligned to 16 as required by System V ABI
            # ref: http://www.x86-64.org/documentation/abi.pdf , page 16
            self.state.regs.rsp = (self.state.regs.rsp & 0xfffffffffffffff0) - 8
```

Reading the document (which now lives at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wiki/hjl-tools/x86-psABI/x86-64-psABI-1.0.pdf), it says

> In other words, the value (%rsp + 8) is always a multiple of 16 (32 or 64) when control is transferred to the function entry point

I believe the key phrase here is "_when control is transferred to the function entry point_", which I read as **after** the `call` instruction. However, in angr's `__libc_start_main` abstraction, the alignment is made **before** the return pointer is placed onto the stack. Thus, when the control is transferred to the function entry point, alignment is off by 8.

Does this seem plausible? If so I'm happy to provide a pull request.

Hello,
by using a softmax on the output, you have changed the distribution, so you need to account for that.
Your problem looks similar to what they do in Soft Actor Critic (https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.01290), see "enforcing action bounds" section.
In the paper, they squash the ouput with a tanh function and used a formula you can find here (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_density_function), in the "Dependent variables and change of variables" section.
However, for your problem, softmax is not bijective (not injective in fact), so it does not seem trivial to adapt to your problem (https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/7164/change-of-variable-with-non-bijective-function).
A suggestion from that last link is to "divide the domain of your non-bijective function into parts where the function is bijective and then apply change of variables.".

Here's my ideal scenario:
- user types in their input
- cmd2 parses the input, stripping comments, looking for pipe redirection, quoted strings, etc
- cmd2 calls the appropriate `do_command` method, passing a ParsedString object (cmd2 already does this) which:
    - looks like a string containing the arguments. This maintains backwards compatibility with `cmd`. Comments have been stripped, as have any redirector and pipe clauses.
    - has a `.command` method which contains the name of the command
    - has a `.arglist` method which gives you a list of arguments just like `sys.argv` does. In this list, quoted arguments are a single element in the list, and the quotes have been stripped.
    - has a `.rawinput` method which gives you exactly what the user typed: comments, redirectors, and everything

This would replace (or diminish the need for) the `@with_arglist` decorator.

Well, I think there is a misconception:

Of course, if any admin just changes the password fielf with ldapmofidy (or using a django-ldapdb field, or whatever), then of course smbk5pwd does not trigger. It triggers on the LDAP Modify Password Extended Operation. This operation is what ldappasswd uses to change the password.

This operation - and ldappasswd, its command-line wrapper - can, however, also be used by an admin to seta new password. As long as the password field is writable by the admin, they can also use the Password Modify operation and ldappasswd.

That's why I keep suggesting that a PasswordField in django-ldapdb calls the Password Modify operation - it is the same as updating the field manually, only that the LDAP server does the right thing, including triggering smbk5pwd.

You can make your own self signed certs using a hook like this:

```
--renew-hook 'cd "$RENEWED_LINEAGE" && openssl req -x509 -key privkey.pem -keyout pubkey.pem -out self-signed.pem -days 365'
```

That's not a complete solution, because `openssl` still asks for things (including the domains) interactively, but you have them available by via the `$RENEWED_DOMAINS` variable so you should be able to `cat` them into an openssl conf file to automate this fully.

This patch does these:

1. Add option `default_env` to all actions. This `default_env`, if specified, has to be a dictionary, and keys defined in this dictionary will only be added to `os.environ` if the key does not already exist.

2. Add option `env` to all actions. This `env`, if specified, has to be a dictionary, and will be used to update `os.environ` to override existing settings.

3. Add action definition option `default_args`, which will add some keyword arguments to user provided options if unspecified. Using this [here](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/src/sos/actions_r.py#L35) will has the following consequences.

1. The usual `R` action
```
R:
  script
```
is equivalent to
```
R: default_env={'R_DEFAULT_PACKAGES': 'datasets,methods,utils,stats,grDevices,graphics'} 
  script
```
which will add `R_DEFAULT_PACKGES` if it is not defined.

2. Users can use
```
R: env={'R_DEFAULT_PACKAGES': 'whatever'}
  script
```
to override environmental definitions.

Please test and let me know if this works as expected.

Yes, the default gnuplot formatter is "%g" which has 6 digits. I am not sure thats the best. 

You should be able to work around this by creating a GnuPlotFormatter and passing 
a different number_format to it such as `.10g` to get 10 digits after the dot. I am not sure what the default should be be but I suspect we should change it.

See https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/data/gnuplot_format.py#L30


```python
In [11]: "%g" %  (numpy.pi)
Out[11]: '3.14159'

In [17]: "%.10g" %  (numpy.pi)
Out[17]: '3.141592654'
```

> With dask, is there any way to prevent constant duplication of a dataset in memory? As it is, it's fairly easy to parallelize TPOT with the python multiprocessing module, but that requires cloning the entire environment with pickle through some IPC. This is a problem for large data-sets, as the memory copying takes up a lot of time, and in the end you need a shit-ton of RAM.

I suspect that Dask's memory management heuristics will handle this without significant issue.

> Intermediate states of the graph are not cached between generations. The solution for this is trickier. Maybe it is possible to build a graph for all generations at the same time, but that would imply to reimplement DEAP as a dask graph and just the idea makes my head spin.

Note that with the newer scheduler you can evolve a computation on the fly, so you don't need to decide everything ahead of time.  The right API to look at for this is probably the [concurrent.futures](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/futures.html) API.  You can submit many tasks, wait for a few of them to come back, and then based on those results submit more that are more likely to be helpful.  Here is a talk at the last SciPy conference that talks about some of the more real-time features of the dask schedulers: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZxNPVTHQwGo

Not necessarily related to this PR, however, for testing correctness, it would be nice to be able to modulate the frequency of flushing. E.g., if we were flushing strictly on a timer, then we could make it very frequent or very infrequent, which could help catch bugs. What do you think about that?

Related to this PR, one category of scheduling bugs that we had over and over was related to scheduling. Basically, we never called `DispatchTasks` on a timer, and so we had to make sure to call `DispatchTasks` in every single event handler that could cause a task to become schedulable (e.g., task submission, worker blocking, worker death, etc). There were many places and it was very easy to miss one, which would result in a schedulable task not executing and so things would hang. Are you worried about the same issue arising for LinageCache flushing?

I've recently done a lot of work on this for HypothesisWorks/hypothesis#200, which now ships with PEP 561 type hints for the public API.  Happy to at least do design and mentoring on this :smile: 

The Zulip post is old but remains an excellent suggestion if you're aiming to annotate the whole codebase, but the inside-out approach will be much more work than needed if we just want to provide hints for downstream users of the Trio public API.  Either is a reasonable choice IMO, and incremental adoption is the best way to start regardless so we can decide later.

Inline annotations are the most idiomatic solution, but sometimes have some issues with circular imports or import time; that's fixable with conditional (`if False:`) imports and forward references for complex types but at the cost of linter issues and being ugly.  I *really* don't like stub files, because they're hard to keep in sync - python/mypy#5028 will fix that but until then IMO they're unsuitable for same-package hints.  Type comments have essentially the same problems as the annotations workaround, with worse linter handling and less runtime metadata.

IMO for Trio annotations are the best solution unless Pypy means we have to use comments.

Once it's clear what our end-goal is, I'll set up an initial config and get CI passing, then it can be open season for incremental contributions.

I guess this is something that the coverage folks have discussed a bit in the past, e.g. thread from 2010: https://groups.google.com/forum/m/#!topic/coveragepy-dev/pKgslISuFjM

We should probably implement the ability to test cancellations before talking to the coverage folks about measuring it, since there's not much point in asking them to implement something that people can't actually use :-).

Probably the request would be (a) a configurable (opt-in) way to treat `await` statements as "branches" that have a "raised Cancelled" branch, and (b) a way to mark particular *functions* as not counting as "cancellable" (so sites that only call those functions wouldn't be considered a cancellation-branch-point even if they use `await`). The main reason this is needed is for async context managers, like `trio.Lock`: acquiring a lock can block, so locks need to be async context managers instead of regular context managers, and then you have to make releasing the lock (`Lock.__aexit__`) marked as async as well, even though it doesn't actually do anything async.

I wasn't suggesting changing the upper bound on sleeping.  You'd merely have a task that does nothing but wake up every 100ms--in effect forcing an upper limit on the sleep time passed to select() and related functions.   On a modern machine, you'd never notice the effect.

Sorry to bug you again. It seems like django's `get_func_args` is not well suited for our usecase. Travis is spitting venom for tox environment `py27-django111`. This might happen because [get_func_args v1.11](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.14/django/utils/inspect.py#L34) slightly changed to [get_func_args v2.0](https://github.com/django/django/blob/2.0/django/utils/inspect.py#L4).

This hurts us because in `py27-dango111` they use `argspec.args` to evaluate the function parameter but they drop the first parameter of each function with `return argspec.args[1:]  # ignore 'self'` (see [get_func_args v1.11](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.14/django/utils/inspect.py#L37)). This makes sense if dealing with object or class methods, but with lambda or casual functions (without a self parameter) this simply drops the "real" first argument. The `Python 3` versions with `django>=2.0` using `inspect.signature` works fine. I don't know if the django guys did this by intention or if this bug. This leads me to the conclusion that if we want to support `py27-dango111`, we have to make a utility function based on [get_func_args v1.11](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.14/django/utils/inspect.py#L34) but without slicing the first argument. I hope you understand what I mean that you might come up with a better idea.
